file_name: ADMA BioManufacturing, LLC -  Amendment #3 to Manufacturing Agreement .txt

perturbation.original_text: ADMA agrees to assume the risks of loss to the Sanofi Pasteur Property and shall reimburse Sanofi Pasteur, at the dollar value, the Source Plasma in case of damage or loss.
perturbation.changed_text: ADMA will endeavor to assume the risks of loss to the Sanofi Pasteur Property and may reimburse Sanofi Pasteur, depending on circumstances, for the Source Plasma in case of damage or loss.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'agrees to assume...and shall reimburse' to 'will endeavor to assume...and may reimburse' introduces ambiguity and makes the obligation non-mandatory. 'Endeavor' implies an attempt, not a guarantee, and 'depending on circumstances' introduces a discretionary element to the reimbursement, weakening ADMA's responsibility for the Source Plasma.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under Florida law, when a seller breaches a contract, the buyer is entitled to damages to put them in as good a position as if the seller had fully performed the contract. By making the reimbursement discretionary, the modified text contradicts this principle, as it allows ADMA to potentially avoid compensating Sanofi Pasteur for the loss of Source Plasma, even if ADMA is responsible for the loss.

F.S. 672.713 672.713 Buyer’s damages for nondelivery or repudiation. — (1) Subject to the provisions of this chapter with respect to proof of market price (s. 672.723 ), the measure of damages for nondelivery or repudiation by the seller is the difference between the market price at the time when the buyer learned of the breach and the contract price together with any incidental and consequential damages provided in this chapter (s. 672.715 ), but less expenses saved in consequence of the seller’s breach. (2) Market price is to be determined as of the place for tender or, in cases of rejection after arrival or revocation of acceptance, as of the place of arrival. History. — s. 1, ch. 65-254. Note. — s. 2-713, U.C.C.
perturbation.justification: The modification weakens ADMA's obligation to reimburse Sanofi Pasteur for lost Source Plasma, potentially allowing ADMA to avoid compensation. The original text states ADMA 'agrees to assume...and shall reimburse,' while the modified text changes this to 'will endeavor to assume...and may reimburse.' This contradicts the principle outlined in Florida Statute 672.713, which states that a buyer is entitled to damages to be put in as good a position as if the seller had fully performed. By making reimbursement discretionary, ADMA could fail to fully compensate Sanofi Pasteur for losses, undermining the principle of making the buyer whole.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties shall use best efforts to complete such amendment to the Quality Agreement within 60 days after the Amendment Effective Date.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties shall consider using efforts to complete such amendment to the Quality Agreement within a reasonable timeframe after the Amendment Effective Date.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'best efforts' with 'consider using efforts' and '60 days' with 'reasonable timeframe' introduces significant ambiguity. 'Consider using efforts' is far weaker than 'best efforts' and doesn't obligate any real action. 'Reasonable timeframe' is subjective and lacks a defined deadline, thus contradicting the intent of establishing a concrete obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statutes § 671.304 implies a duty of good faith in the performance of a contract. The original 'best efforts' clause reinforces this duty, requiring the parties to act diligently and earnestly. The modified text significantly weakens this obligation, potentially allowing a party to delay or avoid amending the Quality Agreement without a genuine, good-faith effort. This undermines the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing.

F.S. 671.304 671.304 Laws not repealed; precedence where code provisions in conflict with other laws; certain statutory remedies retained. — (1) The article on documents of title (Art. 7) does not repeal or modify any laws prescribing the form or contents of documents of title or the services or facilities to be afforded by bailees, or otherwise regulating bailees’ businesses in respects not specifically dealt with herein; but the fact that such laws are violated does not affect the status of a document of title which otherwise complies with the definition of a “document of title” (s. 671.201 ). (2) The following laws and parts of laws are specifically not repealed and shall take precedence over any provisions of this code which may be inconsistent or in conflict therewith: (a) Chapter 517 — Sale of securities. (b) Chapter 687 — Interest and usury; lending practices. (c) Chapter 516 — Florida Consumer Finance Act. (d) Chapter 520 — Retail installment sales (Part I, Motor Vehicle Sales Finance Act; Part II, Retail Installment Sales Act; Part III, Installment Sales Finance Act). (e) Chapter 665 — Florida Savings Association Act. (f) Chapter 657 — Credit unions. (g) Chapters 319 and 328 — Title certificates (motor vehicle; vessel). (h) Chapter 538 — Secondhand Dealers and Secondary Metals Recyclers. (i) Chapters 658, 660, and 663. (3) The following laws or parts of laws, although not repealed, shall yield to and be superseded by any provisions of the code which may be inconsistent or in conflict therewith: (a) Chapter 697 — Instruments deemed mortgages and the nature of a mortgage. (b) Chapter 701 — Assignment and cancellation of mortgages. (c) Chapter 702 — Foreclosure of mortgages. (d) Chapter 727 — General assignments. (4) Notwithstanding any provisions to the contrary in any of the following Florida Statutes, the remedies provided by such statutes shall not restrict the remedies otherwise available to a secured party under this code, but all such remedies shall be cumulatively
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity that weakens the original obligation. The original text uses 'best efforts' and '60 days,' creating a clear expectation of diligence and a specific timeframe. The changed text, however, replaces this with 'consider using efforts' and 'reasonable timeframe,' which are significantly weaker and lack defined deadlines. The Law Explanation suggests that Florida Statutes § 671.304 implies a duty of good faith, which the original 'best efforts' clause reinforces. By weakening the obligation to 'consider using efforts,' the modified text undermines the implied covenant of good faith, potentially allowing a party to delay or avoid amending the Quality Agreement. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to F.S. 671.304, suggesting this section is relevant. The weakening of the obligation is a clear move away from what could be considered a good-faith effort. This change creates a contradiction because the original text established a concrete obligation, while the modified text creates a vague and unenforceable one.

perturbation.original_text: Shipment of the Source Plasma to Sanofi Pasteur shall be made in compliance with the transportation conditions as provided in the Quality Agreement (as defined below), to be further amended by the Parties as contemplated in Section 9 below.
perturbation.changed_text: Shipment of the Source Plasma to Sanofi Pasteur should ideally be made with consideration for the transportation conditions mentioned in the Quality Agreement (as defined below), as further amended by the Parties as contemplated in Section 9 below.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall be made in compliance with' to 'should ideally be made with consideration for', the obligation to strictly adhere to transportation conditions is weakened. 'Should ideally' suggests a preference rather than a requirement, and 'consideration for' is far less stringent than 'compliance with'. This introduces ambiguity regarding the required level of adherence to the transportation conditions specified in the Quality Agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under Florida contract law, a material breach occurs when a party fails to perform a substantial part of the contract. Strict adherence to transportation conditions for Source Plasma is likely material due to the product's nature. The modified text allows for deviations, increasing the risk of damaged or compromised plasma. If the plasma is damaged due to non-compliance, the weakened obligation may make it difficult to establish a material breach, potentially hindering Sanofi Pasteur's ability to seek remedies for the loss or damage of the Source Plasma, and thus contradicts principles defining material breaches.

perturbation.justification: The original contract states 'shall be made in compliance with,' creating a clear obligation to adhere to transportation conditions. The changed text uses 'should ideally be made with consideration for,' which weakens this obligation. The LLM explanation points out that under Florida contract law, failure to perform a substantial part of the contract constitutes a material breach, and strict adherence to transportation conditions is likely material. The modified text increases the risk of damaged plasma due to non-compliance. Therefore, the shift from 'shall' to 'should ideally' and 'compliance' to 'consideration' creates ambiguity and weakens the original legally binding requirement, which is considered a contradiction.
